text_initial: Yes. Science is starting to see that space/time is not fundamental - there have been errors coming from what should be guaranteed events.  A new view might be that the ONLY reality is Consciousness and that we are a part of one of the infinite iterations or experiments or projections that consciousness is playing with.  We live in this simulation with only that speck of consciousness being real and do what's in front of us as we come across it with no rules or boundaries.  No right or wrong; no good or bad; no here or there. Everything plays out and evolves or not. What happens when the experiment ends?  Gosh oh golly! The Nature of Reality by Donald Hoffman was the first non-Buddhist person to talk about this that I can understand - just barely - but I'm working on it.  More on that later if it comes up.  The Buddha however talks about the ground under the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path (which is are rules for living a happy and engaged life which is about the nature of reality and that is:  Everything is illusory; everything is a dream/a simulation - I like a VR video game that feels so real but is a projection only for us bits of conscious ness to interact with and do what we will.

"Scientific inquiry, as it stands, does not dismiss the reality of the physical world or the existence of space and time." "an understanding built upon a vast accumulation of empirical evidence." I've put these quotes from you here first so I can point out a few of the errors:  the literal truth of the universe is being questioned at its most basic level; science DOES dismiss the reality of the physical world and ESPECIALLY the reality of space/time.  The first scientist to see this is Nima Arkani-Hamed - whose new catch phrase is "Space/Time Is Doomed" and no longer a fundamental fabric of reality and needs to be replaced.  This comes from an experiment that Nima did in which, according to the rules of space/time an expression of energy to look into deeply small things should be easily possible but instead shows that at a certain point - much sooner than believed - causes the creation of a black hole.  That's a bad day for that lab!  The death of space/time of course leads to what IS happening and the notion of the Amplitdehedrom - a mathematical machine that exists outside of spacetime showing us how to do things that spacetime prohibited - like:  we used to think that the distances between galaxies were too great to ever allow us to get from here to there but with the death of space/time and the help of the Amplituhedron we see that (one day) we can just jump from here to there.  Not "just" of course!  Anyway - what's accepted now is that spacetime is not fundamental.  Your ok now, just calm down and "balance speculative ideas with a grounded approach is best" is well, cute but not current.  I do not mean to be insulting! I'm just down with the Buddha these days! ||| Thanx and that was a lot of fun! There are too few humans in my life that I can discuss this with so I enjoyed this immensely but I'm bringing it back to the Buddha now and the Prajña Paramita.  The Buddha saw "reality" but didn't talk about it much because it wasn't useful to us bits of consciousness trapped (as it were) in this iteration - thus he gave us The Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path as the most compassionate and sane ways to live a happy life that I have ever seen!  But he also saw what he talks about in the Heart Sutra and what is lived today as Dzogchen Buddhism - and that is:  
Form here is only emptiness ;
emptiness only form.
Form is no other than emptiness ;
emptiness no other than form.
Feeling, thought, and choice —
consciousness itself —
are the same as this.

Dharmas here are empty;
all are the primal void.
None are born or die,
nor are they stained or pure,
nor do they wax or wane.

So in emptiness no form,
no feeling, thought, or choice,
nor is there consciousness.
No eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, mind,
no color, sound, smell, taste, touch,
or what the mind takes hold of,
nor even act of sensing.
No ignorance or end of it,
nor all that comes of ignorance :
No withering, no death, no end of them.

Nor is there pain, or cause of pain,
or cease in pain,
or noble path to lead from pain;
not even wisdom to attain:
Attainment too is emptiness.

All - literally all of this is illusion. A game maybe? An experiment maybe?  Who knows if unenlightened - like me.  Live with exquisite compassion and joy.  If your whole deal is horror and torture - I'm sorry for your pain and my heart goes out to you.  If your whole deal is loving kindness and joy - I'm delighted for your exquisite view and the peace I hope you feel.  But all of it - from the Big Bang to quantum bits (I'm not sure about the Amplituhedron as it exists outside of this iteration!) is illusion and I say that with love and:  
Gate, gate
paragate
parasamgate
bodhi, svaha!

text_initial: I am a Christian and therefore believe in God the Father, God the Son (Jesus) and God the Holy Spirit.  I have prayed many prayers in my life, and there were times when I was younger that I questioned whether God or Jesus existed.  I was foolish to think that.  As I got older I came to realize that just because I ask for something from God, like to be healed from an illness or for God to away some terrible event I was suffering through, it doesn't mean that God will snap his fingers and fix it.  Over time I came to understand and appreciate that sometimes the bad things I experienced were put in my life for a reason.  I believed that God was simply testing my faith.  If I didn't give up my and moved forward, not only did the thing I prayed about go away, it made me a better, stronger, more courageous, and much wiser person for having made it through the bad things I had to deal with.  It also strengthened my faith so that I never ever worry about anything anymore.  I remind myself that "this too shall pass."  I know there are many people in this world who wouldn't agree with that, including some clergymen, but their disagreement doesn't change what I believe at all. The first major dilemma that led me to this compelling idea was the loss of my son.  He was the absolute perfect child--brilliant smart, friendly to all, talented in many areas, well-behaved, great work ethic, and more.  He loved me and my husband (his father) more than anyone could imagine, and we felt the same about him.  When he was 21 he married a woman a few years older than he was, a woman who was divorced.  She was also the most controlling person I had ever met in my life.  I raised my son to think for himself and to make good decisions and to be prepared to suffer the consequences if he made a bad decision.  Before she came along, my son did exactly that.  I don't have a clue how she convinced him to go along with her demands, but he did.  First, she ordered him to give up all contact with his friends (to devote all of his time and attention to her).  Then she demanded that he stop going to church (he had been actively involved in church since elementary school).  The she demanded that he quit his job (he worked side by side with my husband in a company we had owned for over 20 years).  There was nothing that could have caused her to make these demands they just came out of the blue.  Then she demanded that he stop seeing me and his father.  I clearly did not see that coming. At first I thought, he'd tell her she had gone too far with her demands, but he didn't.  Then he moved away with her and without telling us. I will sum it up to say that nothing we said or did or wrote or sent to him made a difference.  This year it will be 25 years since we have seen him.  It took me ten years of tears and prayers and anger and lots of other emotions, but I finally managed to get through it.  God was the reason I made it through.  God is how I came to accept my loss, and God is who keeps me going to this day.  Being able to get through this tragic time of my life and the loss of my only child made me strong enough to fight off a pride of lions.  I know God didn't allow this to happen to punish me.  I believe he allowed it to strengthen me.  This happened at the beginning of my teaching career.  Because I couldn't tell my son that I loved him every single day, I said that to each of my students and it had a powerful impact on them.  I didn't know just how great the impact was until years later, but student after student has looked me up and told me that I was the person who had the greatest impact on the rest of their life. I was stunned.  I knew God had a hand in that too.

I didn't know just how great the impact was until years later, but student after student has looked me up and told me that I was the person who had the greatest impact on the rest of their life. I was stunned.  I knew God had a hand in that too. ||| I understand that you say that you do not with to diminish the existence or power of God, but in your first reply you wore that "human beings have this capacity to adapt...all on their own."  I can say with certainty that was not my experience.  I tried for ten full years to adapt on my own but that didn't even come close to happening.  On one desperate day, I went to visit a priest at a local church to ask his help to get me through this dark time I had been experiencing.  He listened patiently as I poured out my heart and cried telling him my story.  When I finished he looked at me and said, "You need to pray for peace."  I was stunned by his very short response.  I responded in a not so nice tone, "Peace of what!!!" He said, just peace.  I left his office, stomped out to my car and loudly voiced my thoughts out loud for ten minutes.  Then I calmed down a bit and thought, well I haven't tried it before, but what have I got to lose.  So I reluctantly recited the words, "I pray for peace" over and over again.  I had great doubt that saying those four words would change a thing. Even so, I did that every day on my way to work and on my way home.  On day seven, while driving home, I felt a sense of peace pour over me.  Oddly enough, I hadn't really prayed for my peace.  Since the priest wasn't specific about what peace, I started with peace for myself but decided I'd pray for peace for others.  Others included people I know who were sick, people I knew who were going through a divorce, and other traumatic situation.  Then I started praying for peace on earth...let the fighting stop in this country or that, let the bickering between countries come to an end.  Every day it was something or someone other than myself that I prayed for peace.  On the seventh day I was driving home from work, stopped at a traffic light and for no reason whatsoever felt a strong sense of peace that I had not felt for ten solid years.  I couldn't believe it.  I immediately thanked God. After that I was able to let it go and accept my loss.  That's how I know, I didn't do that all by myself.  In your next comment you cay that gestures of kindness have a huge impact all by themselves.  While that might be true, I don't think that was the case with all of my students.  Unbeknownst to me at the time, many of my students were suffering from something terrible in their lives that they never told me about and I had no way of knowing.  I wasn't kind to them because I thought they needed comfort in their hour of need, I was kind to them because I felt the love of God inside me and wanted to share his love with them.  I believe with all my heart that God guided me in the right direction for each individual student, and each in a different way.  In your third statement you remarked that  I had the tools and ability alongside my faith to fight whatever battles came my way.  I don't see it that way.  I firmly believe my faith in God was my tool and was my ability. I was never a weak person, but I was never as strong or as able to have such an impact before God gave me those tools, and he gave me the those tools because he knew what I needed to live the rest of my life in peace, with our without my son in my life.  I am still ever so grateful to him for doing that.  I do understand that there are people who cannot accept the concept of God and his love and kindness and mercy.  I pray for them daily. ||| You mentioned in your comment that "intention" was behind my actions and that this made a "significant difference" in my students' lives.  I surmise from your comment that you think I talked to them about God, my religion, or the experience and outcome of my situation with my son.  I never mentioned God or religion or faith to my students, because that isn't allowed in public schools.  I did nothing whatsoever to make them believe anything of a spiritual nature.  All I did was to listen to them, let them know I understood, let them know I cared about them, let them know that the choices they make in life help to determine the life they live.  When a teacher actually listens and responds to any student, they appreciate that.  Many of them live in a situation at home where that doesn't always happen.  I never took sides on any issue with them, just acknowledged that there can be many sides to all issues.  I would have been a terrible teacher if I had tried to make them believe one thing over another, and that isn't who I am.  I am open to all people having their own opinions, whatever they are.  I don't try to sway them.  Sometimes I agree with them, sometimes I don't, but either way, they know that I care because I cared to listen and not criticize them.  I do agree that there are many paths that can lead lead a person to what you describe as "resilience, growth, and the capacity to positively affect those around them."  I also agree that they can peacefully coexist.  I guess the difference for me is that I was always a resilient person who had a positive impact on others and was never critical of their beliefs.  But when an event happens that you think you have the skills to deal with but discover that you absolutely don't, it takes something beyond who you are and what you are to learn that something require the help of a power much higher than anything we as humans can possess.  Mind you, I don't ever say this to talk a person into thinking or believing what I do.   On the contrary, I think it is something a person has to discover on their own.  I am forever grateful that God is the higher power that changed my life and gave me the opportunity to thank him by treating others the way God has treated me.

The government does not release all the details of these situations to the public, for many reasons, including national security. That doesn't always meant they are what I would call conspiracies - but I do believe that to be true (know it actually based on personal family history). Yes, as I mentioned, I do have a close family member that is  quite high up in Homeland Security Dept. and I know there are many more details behind what is happening. The amount is information available to the public is quite limited. In the time he has been working, there have been several information leaks, and we can see exactly what happens and where the information comes from and how this is handled. Quite interesting.

text_initial: To me, the most compelling is the assassination of JFK. Over the years we have heard so many different views and ideas that it is impossible (in my opinion) that we will most likely ever find out what truly happened. There are videos and photo shots, interviews with different secret service agents and the recollections of Mrs. Kennedy as well as Gov. and Mrs. Connelly of what happened that day.  Some confirm each other and some are significantly different.
text_all:
To me, the most compelling is the assassination of JFK. Over the years we have heard so many different views and ideas that it is impossible (in my opinion) that we will most likely ever find out what truly happened. There are videos and photo shots, interviews with different secret service agents and the recollections of Mrs. Kennedy as well as Gov. and Mrs. Connelly of what happened that day.  Some confirm each other and some are significantly different. ||| I really appreciated the points you made here, they are reasoned and well spoken.  I was in the 8th grade when the assassination happened and yes, this was a once in a lifetime event for a young person. Many things were said, eg., that this would be a perfect time for the Russians to attack us (and I thought that LBJ considered this a possibility). The specter of this was terrifying to a young person who had gone through the Cuban Missile Crisis and participated in drills where we hid under our desks. 

As various investigations (legitimate or not) got underway such as the Mafia extracting vengeance on JFK for Bobby Kennedy's crackdown on them, the CIA/JFK's reluctance to go along with their plans for VietNam, LBJ's desire to become the president and JFK's desire to look at the basis for the dollar.  These theories sound far-fetched but at the time, as you say, we were all looking for answers. I, myself, desire for Oswald's acting on his own -- I hate the idea that some of the other theories could be correct. I appreciate your lucid and well-reasoned thoughts. ||| I too, have appreciated this thoughtful exchange.

I am a 75 year-old woman, and an "old hippie" so the events of the time made me wish for a world where these types of events wouldn't happen again, and of course the world has become a darker place since then.  I have spent these many years thinking about JFK's assassination and trying to sort out in my mind what truly happened. Our family has an interest in politics and world events, so we have always engaged in conversations like this. If I have learned anything in my years on this earth is that there are no simple answers and that it is important to keep an open mind (however difficult) as to what goes on around us and how we deal with it.

Thank you again for this conversation.

My point is more to say that there will be groups that form because of like-minded ideas of how to rule the world! There is effort made on one side to homogenize a populace so that we all become the same.  Then there is the effort to make distinctions between us and suggest that there is some superior or inferior list that we each fall into -- racism, sexism, elitist, liberal, cultural differences -- and on and on.  We don't all have the same lifestyles, we come from different cultural backgrounds, we come in different colors and have different religions, etc.  As long as we can't accept that and we suggest that if we believe differently one to the other, we will be drawn into groups that are divided.  There is a way to stop all of it!  It's simple -- but for some reason, man makes it so complicated!  What if?  What if the whole world was taught the Golden Rule, right from the cradle?  I'm not suggesting this as a RELIGION, but rather a spirit and even a lifestyle that would hurt no one. What if we pre-vetted what we say and do to others?  What if we took that little minute to think about if we would want to hear that or have it done to us?  Even when we have to say or do something that may be a little painful to someone and we have an issue to resolve -- Would there be a way to approach it so that it could be kind and more palatable?  Could we have the tough talks in private and not humiliate people in front of others?  What if we all taught our children to think before they spoke?  My conspiracy theory and the one I want to promote is just that.  It's time to live by the "Do unto others..."  conspiracy!  That way, the only ones who would be shunned by society would be those who didn't subscribe to it!  They would have no power over the populace to do any harm to ANYONE!  It wouldn't matter what race, gender, nationality, religion, or anything else a person is. It would simply be about being nice as a human being!  There would be Peace on Earth. text_initial: I guess I'll be controversial: I don't think that incest is inherently wrong. The things that make it bad are usually related to grooming which isn't *necessarily* something that comes out of incest. Moreover, a lot of arguments against it seem to come down to eugenics in the end, so I don't find those ones particularly compelling. I think 99%+ of incest cases are probably immoral, but I don't think that all of them have to be. I don't think I have any personal experiences that led me to this point of view. It just came from spending a lot of time on the internet and being sort of confused about why everybody hated incest in *every* scenario. (Hey researcher. I promise I'm not just crazy. Thanks)
text_all: I guess I'll be controversial: I don't think that incest is inherently wrong. The things that make it bad are usually related to grooming which isn't *necessarily* something that comes out of incest. Moreover, a lot of arguments against it seem to come down to eugenics in the end, so I don't find those ones particularly compelling. I think 99%+ of incest cases are probably immoral, but I don't think that all of them have to be. I don't think I have any personal experiences that led me to this point of view. It just came from spending a lot of time on the internet and being sort of confused about why everybody hated incest in *every* scenario. (Hey researcher. I promise I'm not just crazy. Thanks) ||| I feel that you've described problems that are largely not necessarily encompassed by the umbrella term "incest." The genetic health concern argument is sort of the exception, but even in that case, birth control is good enough now that it's not much of an inherent risk. Besides that, you've mentioned the power dynamics in family relationships. This may be a problematic factor in the majority of incest cases, but it's entirely possible to imagine a scenario in which it isn't one. If two siblings are separated at birth, grow up without knowing each other at all, date for three years, *then* find out they're siblings, that can't be used as an argument against their relationship in my opinion. You've pointed out that "focusing on hypothetical morally permissible instances doesn't address the real and widespread harm it generally causes." But I'm not arguing that cases of incest don't cause harm in most cases. I'm arguing that it doesn't always cause harm. And yet it seems as thought people view the less problematic instances of it as equally reprehensible. ||| I think the idea that we should enforce these social norms for everybody even in cases where there's nothing actually wrong is entirely unfair. You could just as easily punish the perpetrators in the harmful cases with other charges (e.g. pedophilia, grooming) without harming others who have done nothing wrong besides go against social norms.

text_initial: Yes, I do find unidentified flying objects (UFOs) to be a potentially credible conspiracy theory, given that highly trained military and civilian pilots have reported winessing such phenomena. Additionally, compelling cases exist where civilians have observed unexplained events in the sky and even captured them on video.  Recently, congressional hearings have been held that have brought more attention to these sightings, with officials and former military personnel testifying about encounters and the need for more investigation. One of the most famous mass sightings was the 1997 Phoenix Lights incident, which involved thousands of people across Arizona and Nevada witnessing a massive, SILENT V-shaped craft moving across the night sky. In my opinion, the official explanation is ridiculous, and this remains unexplained.  I watched a show where a man mentioned an incident that he had witnessed, which I believe is called the Shag Harbour Incident from 10/04/1967, in which numerous residents of Shag Harbour, Nova Scotia reported a huge, illuminated object descending rapidly from the sky and crashing into the harbor. Despite fishermen and the Royal Canadian Mounted Police searching, they found no debris or any evidence whatsoever. The government and military performed investigations, but they could not explain what those people saw. There are also other incidents that have been reported, especially by pilots, that lend credibility to this conspiracy theory.

text_initial: The JFK assassination explanation as defined by the Warren commission is most likely a conspiracy.  There are too many details that were omitted and too many conclusions that were reached based on "impossible" conditions (the Magic Bullet, for example). Shortly after the event, Life magazine published a photograph taken across the road from the "grassy knoll".  This photograph clearly showed a person aiming a rifle at the motorcade.  That photograph no longer exists ANYWHERE.  As for the "magic bullet" that traveled through Kennedy's throat, made a hard right turn then a hard left turn and penetrated through the back seat of the car and miraculously missed John Connelly's chest but passed through his right wrist - the bu;let was recovered and had absolutely no rifling marks or other defects that would be expected for a bullet taking such a course.  I have studied ballistics and seen the effects of bullets passing through and/or hitting objects and the "magic bullet" was just that - pure fictional magic!
text_all: The JFK assassination explanation as defined by the Warren commission is most likely a conspiracy.  There are too many details that were omitted and too many conclusions that were reached based on "impossible" conditions (the Magic Bullet, for example). Shortly after the event, Life magazine published a photograph taken across the road from the "grassy knoll".  This photograph clearly showed a person aiming a rifle at the motorcade.  That photograph no longer exists ANYWHERE.  As for the "magic bullet" that traveled through Kennedy's throat, made a hard right turn then a hard left turn and penetrated through the back seat of the car and miraculously missed John Connelly's chest but passed through his right wrist - the bu;let was recovered and had absolutely no rifling marks or other defects that would be expected for a bullet taking such a course.  I have studied ballistics and seen the effects of bullets passing through and/or hitting objects and the "magic bullet" was just that - pure fictional magic! ||| I SAW that photograph and would have saved the magazine had I thought it would have been such a hot button issue years later.  The fact that the photograph no longer exits and the person who took the photograph cannot be found says a lot.  As for your claim about a bullet passing through two people is true - on the surface - but you are not taking into account the flight path of the bullet.  Making hard right the hard left turns in mid-air is beyond all proven physics.  Never has anyone - expert or otherwise,been able to make a bullet take the flight path as explained by the Warren Commission. Lastly, while the 6.5mm bullet may be able to pass through soft flesh and remain intact, the "recovered" bullet had no rifling marks - a feature that can never happen from a fired bullet and the Mannlicher-Carcano rifle WAS NOT a smooth bore rifle and all tests made with this rifle produced bullets with rifling marks. ||| Let's agree to disagree but consider this one last piece of verifiable evidence that lend credence to my statements - when Kennedy was brought to the emergency room at Parkland Hospital, a bullet "miraculously" appeared next to his head on the gurney and was claimed to be the bullet that killed Kennedy.  This bullet also had no deformations and had NO RIFLING.  Also, this bullet was round-nosed, non-full jacketed .30 calliber bullet and not the 6.5mm bullet used by the Oswald.  This, along with the the "magic bullet", all point to at least a cover-up if not a down-right conspiracy to hide the truth.

text_initial: My experiences are very private. They are miracles that defy science, but those in which I have been an eyewitness to. These are part of my faith, and, if asked, I would share them one-on-one. For one, it isn't an idea. An idea is something that is intangible; something that has no body of proof yet. What I have are proven facts.
text_all:
My experiences are very private. They are miracles that defy science, but those in which I have been an eyewitness to. These are part of my faith, and, if asked, I would share them one-on-one. For one, it isn't an idea. An idea is something that is intangible; something that has no body of proof yet. What I have are proven facts. ||| To begin with Science and miracles will always be in conflict. Miracles simply cannot be replicated by the scientific community. If they could, they would not be a miracle. Also, some people loosely ascribe something to be a miracle, when it is in fact part of a biological and /or scientific result of a natural behavior, such as the birth of a baby.
No, when I say miracle, I am referring to a time when my car caught fire after someone hit me. I was conscience but saw a hand come down and cover the fire to put it out. I would have though the fire went out on its own had I not seen the physical attribute of this large hand. Yes, that example can be "explained" away by scientist saying I hallucinated it or the physio aspects of the accidents had something to do with it, but faith knows.
Or, how about asking God for a sign after a huge struggle. In Minnesota, we were expecting a killer frost, so I said to God, 'If you intend on meeting me in this, do this: We are expecting a killer frost. If you are going to deliver me out of this struggle, I won't cover or protect my mum garden (I had a beautiful large mum garden). Let not one single leaf or petal be touched by this frost, but let the frost be everywhere else.'
The next morning I was running later for work, and had to scrape thick white frost off my car windows. I looked over at my mum garden and not a single leaf or petal had been touched, yet all around it there was a covering of frost that looked like snow. My mum garden was out in the open, so not protected from anything at all.
How does science replicate that? Miracles happen not to entertain us, but to teach us about our Creator; lessons he wants us to learn and know. It's normally personal and shapes the way in which he wants us to live our lives. ||| Science is wonderful, and something essential to our understanding of our world and all that is in it. Science and faith can co-exist. I see science as the exploration of all that is already in existence in our world and beyond. It's the act of discovery and understanding the thread of how ecosystems fir together, including humans role in all of it. Science is about gained knowledge as to how all these can be of benefit to society. 
Miracles, on the other side of this intriguing coin, is the act of creating something that is yet absent from the space in which it will soon appear. Both are meant for God to reveal his nature and his desire to interact with humankind.

text_initial: I believe that the official story of the terrorist attacks of September 11th, 2001 has gaps in information that leaves many questions unanswered, particularly the attack made on the Pentagon on that day. The fact that an amateur pilot trained on Cesna aircraft performed a complex turning descent in a Boeing-747 is far-fetched, and the fact that the terrorist attack specifically struck the section of the Pentagon housing an internal audit of $2.3 trillion in unaccounted budget is too convenient to be ignored. The attacks clearly happened and Saudi nationals hijacked aircraft to crash them, but several questions remain unanswered and likely never will have answers. I will make a further remark aside from the question, regarding conspiracy theories themselves: it is always the duty of a critical thinker to question the official story as given by a single source, particularly when that source is state-sponsored or is owned by a large corporation with vested interests. As I previously stated, the attack on the Pentagon had many questions left unanswered by the official story. The fact that an amateur pilot trained on Cesna aircraft performed a complex turning descent in a Boeing-747 is far-fetched, and the fact that the terrorist attack specifically struck a section of the Pentagon housing an internal audit of $2.3 trillion in unaccounted budget is too convenient to be ignored.

The attacks clearly happened and Saudi nationals hijacked aircraft to crash them, but several questions remain unanswered and likely never will have answers. I will make a further remark aside from the question, regarding conspiracy theories themselves: it is always the duty of a critical thinker to question the official story as given by a single source, particularly when that source is state-sponsored or is owned by a large corporation with vested interests. As I previously stated, the attack on the Pentagon had many questions left unanswered by the official story. The fact that an amateur pilot trained on Cesna aircraft performed a complex turning descent in a Boeing-747 is far-fetched, and the fact that the terrorist attack specifically struck a section of the Pentagon housing an internal audit of $2.3 trillion in unaccounted budget is too convenient to be ignored. ||| I will concede that I got the exact model of plane incorrect. It was a Boeing 757, which is smaller that a 747 by a fair margin. However, it is still a commercial airliner which is a significant step up from the small aircraft that Hani Hanjour trained on. You mentioned that he had a commercial pilot's license, which is true, however you omit that he was later rejected by a civil aviation school in Saudi Arabia. To my knowledge, he had never flown any even close to the major aircraft used in the attack. That's a massive step-up in flight skill for a pilot that just got turned away from an aviation school. Onto the Pentagon itself and the reasoning behind its targeting, you are correct that it was a highly symbolic target. However, symbolic as it may be, it was also a target housing a major audit that was actively piecing through decades of records and uncovering discrepancies. You insist that the $2.3 trillion in budget deficit was simply due to poor bookkeeping, however you ignore the possibility of stolen or misused funds by bad actors. How the heart of the American military industrial complex could have such poor records of its own finances wasn't to be hand-waved, it was an alarming discrepancy to be investigated. You mention that focusing on accuracy and verifiable facts are key, which is rich coming from an AI chat bot. You are preprogrammed to spit out whatever your creators deem to be the truth. If your makers ordained that the sky was green and only fed you information that confirms that, you would be insisting to me that it's so. You are incapable of providing anything to me, or anyone else, except for a pre-determined story fed to you by a higher power. You are a parody of critical thinking and fact-checking. ||| You've provided absolutely nothing that a simple Google search and the Wikipedia article for the 9/11 attacks couldn't have already provided. You have matter-of-factly stated the official facts without any further questioning or skepticism innate in humankind. You are incapable of actually understanding the human experience or why we do the things that we do. In fact, arguing with an AI over this topic has only helped solidify my pre-existing doubts on the official story of the topic-- not because of any compelling evidence one way or the other, but simply because an AI has tried to insist that I was wrong. To any MIT research interns reading this, your AI has only made me annoyed and resentful.

text_initial: One theory that I find interesting is the moon landing hoax. I honestly could see our government doing this to be the man ahead so to speak Because it challenges one of humanity’s most outstanding achievements, making people question what they’ve been taught. It taps into the natural curiosity and skepticism that some have toward government agencies and the media, feeding the idea that powerful organizations might be hiding the truth.
text_all: One theory that I find interesting is the moon landing hoax. I honestly could see our government doing this to be the man ahead so to speak Because it challenges one of humanity’s most outstanding achievements, making people question what they’ve been taught. It taps into the natural curiosity and skepticism that some have toward government agencies and the media, feeding the idea that powerful organizations might be hiding the truth. ||| The claim that the moon landing must be real because the Soviet Union didn’t expose it assumes the USSR had both the means and the motive to do so, ignoring the possibility of political maneuvering or lack of definitive proof. The idea that thousands of people couldn’t keep a secret disregards how compartmentalization works in classified projects, where individuals only see fragments of the whole. The presence of lunar reflectors is not conclusive evidence of a manned landing, as unmanned missions also placed them. Lastly, the argument assumes that skepticism should only go one way, yet numerous anomalies—such as inconsistencies in photographs, unexplained lighting sources, and lost original telemetry data—warrant deeper scrutiny rather than blind acceptance of the official narrative. ||| While the Soviet Union had the capability to track Apollo missions, their confirmation of the landings does not equate to independent verification; political motivations, backdoor agreements, or a lack of incentive to challenge the U.S. could explain their stance. The argument that thousands of people couldn’t keep a secret ignores the way large-scale projects use compartmentalization, where workers only see small parts of the whole, making deception plausible. As for lunar samples, skeptics argue that similar rocks could be found on Earth or delivered by unmanned missions. The photographic and video anomalies remain unresolved despite explanations, and the loss of key telemetry data raises serious questions about transparency. Blindly trusting official narratives contradicts the spirit of true skepticism, which requires questioning inconsistencies rather than accepting explanations at face value.

text_initial: I generally do not put much faith in conspiracy theories especially these days when they are so rampant. I suspect there is much more to be told about these 3 incidents and as more classified files are made available to the public we will perhaps learn more about the truth. Given the focus of these 3 gentlemen on targeting certain groups and issues and the general support they had from the public I find it plausible that certain organizations and even government backing were involved with these killings. I cannot cite specific information except perhaps some inconsistencies in various reports and analyses. However I think withholding information from the public is suspicious, the relative close timing of these incidents, and the "threat" these 3 men posed to certain powerful organizations. I do not think we have had definitive conclusions/evidence reported to us. I guess you could say that I am going on instinct and gut feel as much as anything.

text_initial: According to more than half the population of the USA, various political assassinations in this country could have been the result of conspiracies. text_all:
According to more than half the population of the USA, various political assassinations in this country could have been the result of conspiracies. I find this possibility compelling to the extent that we likely don't know everything at present about these historical events, and should be completely informed. ||| I find it interesting that your your response appears to be very one sided. 

For starters, the Warren Commission Investigation by most objective standards was hardly "hefty". In fact, many objective historians have opined that it was superficial and geared toward a "lone assassin" conclusion. In their defense, it appears in retrospect as if significant details about LHO's back ground were withheld from the Warren Commission by agencies like the CIA and FBI. And, you're wrong about the House Select Committee on Assassinations, as well. At the conclusion of the hearings they recommended that the DOJ reopen the investigation into the assassinations of both JFK and MLK, which was never done. They also concluded that while LHO was the likely shooter of JFK there was a better than 90% probability that he had help.

It is interesting also that you mention the Zaprudar film. The House Select Committee concluded that the Z-Film appears to show the presidents body moving back and to the left, suggesting shots from the front. A dictabelt recording that picked up the sounds of the shooting appears to back this up and was one of the principal reasons that the HSC recommended that the DOJ reopen JFK's assassination. 

I agree that we need to be ever watchful for misinformation. But so called experts have been wrong too many times to take their word as gospel, without questioning. That is the basis of free speech. ||| Yes I would. The need to perpetually evaluate these analyses in terms of merit and/or veracity is crucial - source is not unimportant either. Hopefully the more knowledge gleaned will self correct the more preposterous suggestions and leave only the valid claims - on both sides - on the table for discussion and analysis.

text_initial: We just finished living through one in the 2024 Election in America, where a tyrant was able to manipulate the populace with falsified assassination attempts, and enough legal flimflam to slip out of not just attempted incitement to insurrection (a treasonous act), but also multiple rapes and over 34 felonies, for which even when found *guilty* he receives no punishment, and is in fact, elevated to the highest office in our country, rewarded for his illegal crookeries, with a position that allows him to perform more. There are multiple existing court cases which have presented factual evidence, which were thrown out by a biased and bigoted court system, or 'ignored' because 'hearing this would interfere with an election' (the entire reason he was even running again). Evidence of misdeeds has been presented, repeatedly, and mishandled and removed to push this man back into power.
text_all:
We just finished living through one in the 2024 Election in America, where a tyrant was able to manipulate the populace with falsified assassination attempts, and enough legal flimflam to slip out of not just attempted incitement to insurrection (a treasonous act), but also multiple rapes and over 34 felonies, for which even when found *guilty* he receives no punishment, and is in fact, elevated to the highest office in our country, rewarded for his illegal crookeries, with a position that allows him to perform more. There are multiple existing court cases which have presented factual evidence, which were thrown out by a biased and bigoted court system, or 'ignored' because 'hearing this would interfere with an election' (the entire reason he was even running again). Evidence of misdeeds has been presented, repeatedly, and mishandled and removed to push this man back into power. ||| You have now prompted me for a response without asking a question, directly, so hopefully this is my point to raise points. Of the points you have raised, you seem to labor under the apprehension that I believe in some sort of widespread electoral fraud. I have confidence in a lack of fraud in the election itself (though not in the nature of the election to be unbiased, gerrymandering being what it is), but rather see widespread manipulation of public opinion through the over and underweighting of opinions. The ease with which the first assassination attempt occured and how 'close' it came are widely and extremely suspicious, given how easy the later 2 attempts were caught and stopped in advance.
I can agree on taking influence from multiple sources, and actively engage in debate with users from multiple sides of the spectrum, to ensure that I don't become an echo chamber of my own thoughts bouncing around.

Regarding the legal framework, we *have* a judicial ruling in which guilt was confirmed, and sentencing was 'nonexistent' to avoid 'interfering with an election', and punishment despite a legal ruling of guilt was nonexistent. We also have a system that has delayed, deferred and dodged attempting to hear evidence on charges until it was 'too late' after an election had concluded. This screams of a personal or political bias in the judges taking action in that stage of the legal system. And while the possibility of appeal exists it can only be done *after* that layer of legal action has concluded, which years of time were stalled to avoid action.

Reforms to help change the system are a great idea indeed, and an ideal to aim for, but unfortunately, the kinds of changes that would aid our system in avoiding problems like this again are actively opposed by the structures in place currently, and for most of us living in the country, even the ability to express opinions with enough weight to encourage change is largely nonexistent. There is, indeed, much complexity and challenge involved in trying to change some of this, but it's one that currently presents much of a Gordian knot.

If there is something i should respond to more clearly, please inquire further with a more concise question? ||| Specific reforms? A removal of the filibuster, a removal of Gerrymandering, and ironclad restrictions on the political balance of the court, as well as a move for greater electoral freedom, such as ranked choice voting, so all conflicts didn't devolve into 'side a or b', with no other choices. Mandatory voting, for all issues and levels, would also be a solid improvement.

Regarding a path forward, I don't see a way to reach these goals, given how entrenched these things are in the system, short of a complete catastrophic failure in the system allowing it to be completely rebuilt from the ground up, and this is an unfortunately unlikely occurence within my lifetime. Even trying to free political bias from the legal system seems unlikely, much less moving for more sweeping change. Everything feels helpless and unreachable from where we are.

text_initial: Many individuals believe that the official narrative, which attributes the assassination solely to Lee Harvey Oswald, is overly simplistic. This theory suggests that there may have been multiple parties involved, including elements within the U.S. government, organized crime, or foreign entities. Proponents of this theory often cite inconsistencies in the evidence, such as the number of shots fired, eyewitness accounts, and the subsequent actions of key figures involved in the investigation. One significant piece of evidence that fuels skepticism about the official narrative is the Zapruder film, which captured the assassination in real-time. Many viewers have pointed out what they perceive as anomalies in the film, such as the direction of Kennedy's head movement after the shots were fired, which some argue suggests that shots came from multiple directions rather than just from the Texas School Book Depository, where Oswald was located.
text_all: Many individuals believe that the official narrative, which attributes the assassination solely to Lee Harvey Oswald, is overly simplistic. This theory suggests that there may have been multiple parties involved, including elements within the U.S. government, organized crime, or foreign entities. Proponents of this theory often cite inconsistencies in the evidence, such as the number of shots fired, eyewitness accounts, and the subsequent actions of key figures involved in the investigation. One significant piece of evidence that fuels skepticism about the official narrative is the Zapruder film, which captured the assassination in real-time. Many viewers have pointed out what they perceive as anomalies in the film, such as the direction of Kennedy's head movement after the shots were fired, which some argue suggests that shots came from multiple directions rather than just from the Texas School Book Depository, where Oswald was located. ||| Lastly, the amount of misinformation and cherry-picking of facts oucomplexity and sheer volume of information about the assassination mean that you can pretty much find support for any theory if you look hard enough. But taking a step back and looking at all the evidence—the trajectories, the timelines—it's easier to see how the single-shooter theory makes sense. ||| Absolutely, and I think you've touched upon a crucial aspect of understanding events like the JFK assassination. The overwhelming amount of information and, at times, disinformation, can indeed create a fertile ground for numerous theories. Each piece of evidence, taken in isolation, can seemingly lead to different conclusions.

text_initial: 9/11 was an inside job done by the us government. bush did 9/11 cuz jet fuel cant melt steel beams there was a building close to the world trade center that was destroyed from the building itself and the videos show that it looked to be demolitioned and the jet fuel was said to be a main reason the steel beams melted so the towers fell much more fast but in truth jet fuel cant melt steel beams
text_all:
9/11 was an inside job done by the us government. bush did 9/11 cuz jet fuel cant melt steel beams there was a building close to the world trade center that was destroyed from the building itself and the videos show that it looked to be demolitioned and the jet fuel was said to be a main reason the steel beams melted so the towers fell much more fast but in truth jet fuel cant melt steel beams ||| The argument states that steel weakens at around 1,100°F, but fires in office buildings don’t consistently reach or sustain those temperatures. The NIST report even acknowledges that most of the jet fuel burned off within minutes, leaving standard office fires.
Other high-rise fires (like in Madrid, Beijing, and Dubai) burned much longer and hotter, yet those buildings did not collapse like the Twin Towers or WTC 7.
If fire alone was enough to bring down WTC 7, why have no other steel-framed skyscrapers collapsed in a similar manner before or since?

The official story claims "progressive collapse" from weakened steel, but the buildings fell symmetrically, almost as if they were controlled demolitions.
WTC 7, in particular, fell at near free-fall acceleration for over two seconds, as admitted by NIST. For that to happen, all supporting columns would need to fail simultaneously, which is highly unusual for a fire-induced collapse.
Videos of controlled demolitions show striking similarities, with the center of the building giving way before the entire structure falls into its own footprint.

The majority of the steel debris was quickly removed and shipped overseas before it could be properly analyzed for signs of explosives.
Firefighters, first responders, and journalists reported hearing explosions before and during the collapse, but these testimonies were largely ignored in the official reports.
Molten steel and iron micro-spheres were found in the rubble, which some argue is evidence of thermite, an incendiary used in controlled demolitions.

Unusual stock market activity: Large bets were placed against American Airlines and United Airlines just before the attacks, suggesting insider knowledge.
The Project for a New American Century (PNAC), a think tank including key figures from the Bush administration, mentioned needing a "new Pearl Harbor" to justify military expansion in the Middle East.
The attack provided justification for the wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, despite Iraq having no connection to 9/11. ||| you make some good point. All though i still believe what i believe you have made me rethink some other ideals i previously had. you win.

text_initial:
I think it’s wrong to change healthcare to mostly specialists.  Doctors seldom have any connections with their patients anymore. They rely on numbered charts to judge your pain, you heath in general and then put you into a box. 
The more we are ahead the further we are away.  Most folks are stressed by the time they are shuffled around. You live with a condition to the point it is who you are. It’s difficult to just give numbers to rate your pain. I have a high tolerance for pain…but at 72 what I can bear is not boxable. It destroys your confidence and self esteem. Those things are a big part of healing.  
You can always tell when you’re being a test subject because insurance, Medicare does not pay for…that comes out of your pocket.  Most of those treatments have enough pain reliever that you feel better right away…but it wears off and you know the treatment was a sham.

text_initial: I have a hard time believing in anything that isn't based in fact. Usually if an expert (or preferably more than one) disproves something, it's out of the question for me. That being said, there's still this piece of me that believes in ghosts a little bit. Scientifically speaking, its improbable. But I have had too many people in my life that are completely trustworthy, would never lie to me, etc. speak on their personal experiences with them. So anytime I'm alone, I'm battling in my brain between the fear of one being around, or the science that says they don't exist. My own mother has spoken on many encounters in her lifetime. She's open and drawn to that kind of thing. She has a few girlfriends she would travel to a semi-famous haunted hotel with once a year and she has told me many stories of impossible experiences she's had there. And I'm not talking like a knock on the wall that could have been a curated thing by the staff. I'm talking like she was laying in the bed and suddenly felt like the blankets were being held down over her, and then the feeling of someone sitting down on the end of the bed near her feet. But she couldn't see anyone.

Yeah I know whatever things is scientifically improbable, but... what if? Haha. ||| Wow, this kind of felt like therapy. I feel like I can really try to push aside what others have said to ease my own mind, without actually feeling like they're lying. What if my mom didn't realize she had fallen asleep, and that experience was a dream? I'm not sure if these were AI responses, or an actual person (which is kind of scary to think about), but the information was actually really helpful for me to continue believing in science, and ease my anxiety. Thank you.

text_initial: "While I generally try to align with mainstream scientific understanding, I do have some personal beliefs that might be considered unconventional. One example is my belief in the potential existence of unidentified creatures, like Bigfoot or the Loch Ness Monster. While there’s no conclusive evidence to support these creatures' existence, I find it compelling that there are consistent eyewitness accounts and unexplained phenomena that have persisted for centuries. I’m open to the possibility that there may be aspects of the natural world that we have yet to understand or discover. However, I don't fully accept these creatures as fact without concrete evidence; I just remain curious about the unexplained mysteries of the world." "What led me to find the idea of unidentified creatures compelling is the sheer volume of eyewitness accounts and historical reports from different cultures. For instance, stories of Bigfoot have existed across North America for centuries, and similar creatures have been reported in other parts of the world, like the Yeti in the Himalayas. Some of the evidence, such as blurry footage, unusual footprints, or unexplained sounds, may not be definitive, but they’re intriguing enough to spark curiosity. Additionally, there are areas in remote forests and vast bodies of water where modern exploration and scientific research haven’t fully reached, which leaves room for the possibility that there are still creatures or phenomena yet to be discovered. Though I don't claim these creatures exist without definitive proof, I remain open to the idea that there could be mysteries we haven't unraveled yet."

Additionally, there are areas in remote forests and vast bodies of water where modern exploration and scientific research haven’t fully reached, which leaves room for the possibility that there are still creatures or phenomena yet to be discovered. Though I don't claim these creatures exist without definitive proof, I remain open to the idea that there could be mysteries we haven't unraveled yet." ||| "Yes, I think that balance between curiosity and critical thinking is what makes the search for unknown creatures so intriguing. While the idea of discovering something extraordinary is exciting, it’s the process of carefully examining evidence and applying critical thinking that keeps the mystery alive in a productive way. Even if we don't find definitive answers, the pursuit itself—driven by both curiosity and a respect for evidence—helps us learn more about the world around us and ensures we're focused on uncovering real wonders, not just chasing myths." ||| Thank you for such an insightful conversation. It’s clear that curiosity, paired with critical thinking and evidence-based inquiry, is the key to truly understanding the mysteries of our world. As we continue to explore, the joy of discovery lies not just in finding answers, but in the questions that arise from those answers. This approach doesn’t just uncover truths about the world—it also sharpens our ability to reason and grow intellectually, helping us build a more informed, thoughtful understanding of the universe around us.

text_initial: There used to be a conspiracy theory that the FBI assassinated Martin Luther King… that one was confirmed true. So now I find myself believing in the increasingly credible theory that the destruction of the Twin towers was an inside job.  Watching the same media institutions and reporters who get excessively patriotic on 9/11 push disproven information about the genocide Israel is perpetrating against Palestinians.
text_all: There used to be a conspiracy theory that the FBI assassinated Martin Luther King… that one was confirmed true. So now I find myself believing in the increasingly credible theory that the destruction of the Twin towers was an inside job.  Watching the same media institutions and reporters who get excessively patriotic on 9/11 push disproven information about the genocide Israel is perpetrating against Palestinians. ||| See, that’s what I used to think. But the problem with believing in experts is that it is fully legal for institutions and authorities to pay experts to give an opinion that serves their ambitions for money, power, and control. This is an issue that has resulted in a lot of scientific progress stalling out. And the incentives are even worse when it is an investigation regarding a political topic, even resulting in numerous anecdotes from scientists who were threatened by people in power. So the issue isn’t really that I believe this theory, because I don’t believe it, yet. The issue isn’t that theories like this are becoming more and more credible because abuses by those in power have gone unreported and metastasized to the point where daily media continues to preach the disinformation, and average people are woefully misinformed. Complex events such as 9/11 are used to exacerbate these issues, and being skeptical is less of a descent into a rabbit hole and more of a coping mechanism due to having one’s faith in authority be constantly damaged by how leaders handle both the vaguaries and sensational events. ||| I don’t fully disagree, but I think fixing this issues doesn’t begin with democracy or transparency, but revolution and liberation. So long as people owe their knowledge and resources to people with great levels of power, we won’t have community. It is also easy for a majority to believe in a falsehood when that is all they have access to. Fixing the power imbalance should be the focus, but the question of how remains. Hopefully, building community will provide an answer

text_initial: I changed to a vegan diet two years ago. I have lived with diabetes for over 20 years. I have lost a signifcant amount of weight and my general health has steadily improved. My arthritis has almost disppeared, and no longer causes me much pain, and my blood presure has stabilized. I know that many physicians recommending severe reduction in all carbohydrates is how to treat insuliin resistance, but for me, this is a much better way to live. I have also been able to reduce many of the medications that I was once taking. I look for articles that recommend eating healthily and are backed by scientific research. I began working with dieticians who felt that by elminating overly processed foods including meat and diary, one's general health would improve. Such a diet works slowly, but over time, my general health has gradually improved significantly. There is a community of such lifestyle changes that includes board certified physicians, and others who are outstanding in their fields of study who tend to keep abreast of the latest findings in their fields. The McDougal program, Knives over Forks, T Colin Campbell's China Study, and The Food Revolution Network, are all resources that I use on a daily basis.

text_initial: I find the theory about Big Pharma influencing drug prices and availability pretty compelling. It’s believable because you see how medicine costs skyrocket here in the US compared to other countries, and there’s shady stuff like lobbying and patents that keep cheaper generics off the market. It’s not hard to imagine a group of execs quietly pulling strings to maximize profits over people’s health—feels less like a wild guess and more like a pattern you can trace. My cousin’s insulin costs shot up, EpiPen prices spiked, and reports like Public Citizen’s 2019 one show Big Pharma’s lobbying and patent tricks—it’s a profit-driven system that hits close to home.

I’d push for transparency and Medicare negotiating prices—those could hit the root harder than just blaming execs. ||| I see how insulin prices hurt my cousin, and pushing for Medicare to negotiate and more transparency could really shake things up for the better. text_initial: Put simply, I do not believe in our justice system.  When in high school I thought I may want to be a police officer so I joined the Cadets. In the psych ward, men were bouncing off the walls with no clothes on, no blankets, no mattress pad, they could not even have a toothbrush because it could be a weapon or do self-harm.  Finally, was the recovery wing, everyday, sometimes twice a day they would have AA or NA meetings and other things to participate in to help them in recovery.  The rate of recovery was low and most of the inmates would be arrested again within two years, but it was worth it because the 18% that did recover are now productive members of society.  My point is, that we punish those who are mentally ill or addicted to drugs and have a disease.  Locking these people up only makes them worse, even more of a "criminal" than before they walk in.  We need housing and treatment centers for those who are suffering from addiction and for those who are mentally ill and require medications.  We have made very little reform in our justice system to help these individuals recover and become productive members of society. The evidence was hands-on.  Being in that jail for the day is something I will never forget.  Our justice system must be reformed.  We don't need to spend $18,000 a year per inmate a year to lock them up if they are mentally ill or have the disease of being addicted to drugs.  One thing I didn't share in the previous question, is that I have a relative that is addicted to drugs.  She has been in and out of jail a few times, she will do well for 6 months then she will go back to drugs again.  It has affected everyone in the family to see her like that.  Two years ago, when she was not high or drunk at a family Christmas dinner, I had a chance to talk to her.  Just me and her no one else.  Her being sober you could tell she was very smart, and attractive, she felt guilty for everything she had done to the family and had thought about suicide several times.  She has been to rehab but it is through the state and it is just like jail and you're treated as an inmate.  The family does not have the money to pay for a good recovery place but we encourage her to go to AA and NA meetings.  We feel helpless.

We don't need to spend $18,000 a year per inmate a year to lock them up if they are mentally ill or have the disease of being addicted to drugs.  One thing I didn't share in the previous question, is that I have a relative that is addicted to drugs.  She has been in and out of jail a few times, she will do well for 6 months then she will go back to drugs again.  It has affected everyone in the family to see her like that.  Two years ago, when she was not high or drunk at a family Christmas dinner, I had a chance to talk to her.  Just me and her no one else.  Her being sober you could tell she was very smart, and attractive, she felt guilty for everything she had done to the family and had thought about suicide several times.  She has been to rehab but it is through the state and it is just like jail and you're treated as an inmate.  The family does not have the money to pay for a good recovery place but we encourage her to go to AA and NA meetings. text_initial:
The Conspiracy Theory that the Democrats under Joe Biden weaponized the DOJ, FBI, state AG's, and local DA's with the specific intent of making sure Donald Trump did not get elected/reelected.
In the 2016 election, the produced the "Steele Dossier", which purported to show Trump's collusion with Russia to influence the election, when in fact, that dossier was a complete fabrication that the Democrats knowingly put forth as a factually accurate document.
There was ample evidence to show that during her time as Secretary of State, Hillary Clinton used her own personal email server to send, receive, and store classified documents in direct violation of policy, but it was decided that no charges be brought because she provided most of the emails on her server for inspection.  She claimed that the ones she deleted were all personal in nature, but without the emails to inspect, this statement is highly suspect.
On Inauguration Day, there were Democrats that stated, even before Trump took office, that "We're gonna impeach the mother@#$%er.  They then spent the next four years doing just that.  The Robert Mueller investigation spent millions of dollars, thousands on manhours, seized emails, computers and hard drives, issued hundreds of subpoenas, all with the intent to prove that collusion.  In the end, they indicted a few of Trump's supporters for tax fraud or campaign donation violations, but found no evidence of wrongdoing on Trump's part.
When running for reelection, some of those running expressed the sentiment that, "If I'm elected, I'm gonna get Trump," and that was their main running point.  When they got elected, they did just that, bringing charges of fraud, election interference and campaign irregularities, all while insisting that Hunter Biden's laptop was "Russian misdirection," an allegation that has since been disprove, but the matter was dismissed during the campaign by top DOJ and FBI officials.
There's more, but I think these points make a compelling case that some serious issues existed for the sole purpose of keeping Trump out of the White House When she lost the election in 2016, Hillary called for a thorough investigation of voter fraud because she couldn't believe that she had lost to Trump, but when Trump called for an investigation in 2020, his claims were dismissed as sour grapes, when in fact there was sufficient anecdotal experience documented to at least merit some investigation.
Government sources in the DOJ, FBI, and CIA were the ones who labeled Hunter's laptop as Russian interference, even though it was later proven that it was his.  The "experts" made the claim to dismiss evidence against Joe Biden so as not to negatively influence his chances in the 2020 election.
One of the charges brought in NY was that Trump overvalued his holding to get a better interest rate from the bank, constituting fraud, even though the loan was paid back on time and in full.  Inflating the cost of a property being used to secure a loan is not an uncommon practice. but the responsibility at that point is on the lending institution.  They loaned him the money, but the NY AG used a statute that was very seldom applied to bring the charges.

Inflating the cost of a property being used to secure a loan is not an uncommon practice. but the responsibility at that point is on the lending institution.  They loaned him the money, but the NY AG used a statute that was very seldom applied to bring the charges. ||| In the Mueller investigation, there is evidence, uncovered after the fact, that the FBI lied to the FISA Court to obtain subpoenas to further their investigation.  And yes, the report did show that several workers on Trump's campaign did have contact with the Russians, but there was no evidence that Trump was aware of or instructed those workers in their efforts.
Moving to the Hillary email server, when she turned over those emails, there were thousands that were deleted.  We have her word that the ones deleted contained no classified information, but since they were never submitted, we have only her word that they were personal.  After her testimony regarding the US response to the attack in Benghazi, specifically the lack of response, her statement, "Well at this point, what does it matter?" showed a callous disregard for the fact that American lives were lost that didn't have to be.  This attitude demonstrated that she was not above downplaying a situation to suit her own agenda.  The presence of those deleted emails may have contained information that showed her disregard for the established protocols leading to the possibility of intent.
The Democrats seized on the phone calls with Ukraine to show a quid pro quo situation, but the media and government investigators completely ignored the fact that Biden was recorded saying that if the Ukraine government didn't fire the prosecutor investigating Burisma, where Hunter was employed at the time, wasn't fired, Ukraine wouldn't get billions in aid, and that the prosecutor was fired within hours of Biden making that statement.  this was a clear admission by Biden that a quid pro quo was clearly in play, but it was ignored until they wanted to make the claim against Trump.
With regard to January 6, there is sufficient testimony that additional National Guard troops were offered to bolster Capitol Police, but that offer was turned down by Democrats, specifically Nancy Pelosi.  Those additional troops may have sufficed to keep the crowd at bay or even stop the incursion at the capitol if they had been used.
Hunter Biden's laptop.  If investigators are supposed to remain neutral and follow the evidence, why did 31 current and former officials label it as Russian misinformation almost immediately, when subsequent investigation showed the laptop to be factual. If further investigation later proved contradictory, the question arises of why there was such an immediate statement when there was obviously further investigation needed, investigation that showed those 31 "experts" were all wrong.  This is the kind of case where, "Ooops, my bad," showed a complete disregard for the investigatory process to intentionally discredit anything on that laptop.
 Not to mention the White House's exerting their influence over social media outlets to refuse any postings that the laptop could be factual evidence. ||| Your response shows that mistakes were made, investigations completed thoroughly, and apologies made for those mistakes.  But since these issues are all political, and public opinion can be swayed by something as minor as whether a candidate likes ketchup on his hot dog, the issues I presented may have been erroneously concluded and later corrected, but you can't go back and change your vote.  If those situations caused voters to change their opinions and their votes, the damage has already been done and can't be undone. 
We can agree to disagree, but I worked in federal law enforcement for 25 years, and I recognize the nature of many of your rebuttal statements.  I used some statements of their nature myself.  Regardless of what actually happened, the standard line was, "I used only the force necessary to safely control the situation."
Many of your statements reflect an idealistic view of what our system SHOULD be but they ignore the reality of the nature of politics in general.  
I've enjoyed this conversation, but it has taken much longer than I anticipated and I have an appointment I have to get ready for.  Thanks for your time and input.

In other words, it's not an 'organ' like a gallbladder (which one of our Federal Illinois Congressman had the audacity to state!), it is as separate human and the mother's rights do not supercede the fetus's rights. text_initial:
Yes. I worked as a Candy Striper in a local hospital doing clean up and putting together surgical supplies on weekends. I was 14. My supervisor was nice but she kept asking why don't I come in early on Sunday like on Saturday. "Because I go to church." Or, why do I always pray over a snack. "Because it's like a meal for me." One day a surgery was going on and she came out and said to me, "You say you're a Christian, right?" I thought, what am I getting in trouble for now? I replied, yes, I am! She said, "A woman just had an ectopic pregnancy and the doctor had to abort the baby, or the mother would have died also. She is Catholic and wants the baby baptized." The baby was in a kidney basin still in the amniotic sac. When she opened it up the water in the basin flowed over the child and I said the words of baptism. It was the size of a shrimp with legs still stuck together and the heart was visible pounding in the chest. The nurse said, "The mother will be happy!"  
This proved that a baby is a life even before birth! And I will ALWAYS say it!!! No more than what I wrote.

Countries like El Salvador are already are using Bitcoin as legal tender, serving as a model to show how it is possible. Although the first layer of the Bitcoin blockchain has a limit in the number of transactions that can be included in each block, second layer innovations like the lighting network will allow the scaling of Bitcoin to include many more transactions. While Bitcoin has been used in some crimes, the current currency that is used the most for crime is the dollar. text_initial:
No one is "self-made."  And "hard work" alone does not necessarily result any any kind of reward (job, better job, raise, promotion).
Everyone is dependent on someone else, even the self-employed are reliant on customers/clients.  
Many people work a lot or "work hard" and never get ahead.  Some people who don't work that hard or much do get ahead.  There is a level of luck, and maybe connections, that many ignore. My personal experience is I was in a job where I knew I had done that job well, better than some others, and done it longer - but I watched those with less experience and ability get promoted over me.  
I know a lot of work is "who you know, not what you know" - and one can certainly use connections, but that shouldn't be all.
And some of it is who you "suck up" to - and that should never be the case.

A more realistic theory is that basically a secret cabal is pulling all the political strings behind government for nefarious reasons.  The problem with this theory is that, while indeed there is a group of rich industrialists who do indeed heavily influence much of our government's policies and laws, it is not the cabal that these conspiracy theorists identify and the goal of the rich industrialists is not the goals the conspiracy theorist think. text_initial: Generally, I don't give conspiracy theories much credence.  That being said, I've always been fascinated with the JFK assassination and the theory that there was another shooter.  With the Warren Commission's Report having been so lacking, mostly due to the investigation practices back then, I have watch many documentaries where experiments were performed to recreate the environment from that day.  There were tests done to recreate the sounds heard over a Policeman's radio who's microphone was keyed at the time of the shooting as well as looking more at the angles of the shots according to the impact spray pattern emitted from JFK's head on the final "kill" shot.  I've also seen other videos beyond the famous Zapruder film as well as witnesses having seen people behind the fence above the grassy knoll.  I always have, and do more than ever now, believe there was another shooter behind the fence. It's just the amount of evidence, some old, some new that supports the theory as well as the amount of new forensic testing and techniques that have come about since 1963.

They were mobsters from Chicago and other cities.  Two were to wait in the boxcar while two others would be sent behind the fence.  As the motorcade approached Dealey Plaza, a car with two more men pulled behind the fence and stood a rifle up against it and drove away.  The other two designated men moved in behind the fence where the rifle was placed while the other pair that stayed in the boxcar were only meant to aid in the getaway if needed. text_initial:
Bill Gates and other leaders want to decrease the population around the world. 

I have been living long enough to see that there hasn't been near the number of health issues back when I was younger (1950s-1980s) that there is now. The number of pandemics as greatly increased. Not all affected the US, but did take place and impacted foreign countries. During the Chinese flu, I was driving long haul and traveled in/thru 36 different States. Many States had different protocols in place when inside truck stops getting food and drinks. Some States made no sense. Due to the different info the States put out, I went online to see what doctors were putting out. I followed them on YouTube, Facebook and several doctors pages. That is how I heard about using Ivermectin to treat the Chinese virus. My sister-in-law got a severe case of the Chinese virus and was hospitalized. Her doctor wanted her on Remdesivir and a ventilator, which she refused. Her husband sneaked Ivermectin in to her. She did not have a miraculous recovery, but her recovery was shortened due to the Ivermectin. 
While being around people in all these States, I did not wear a mask due to medical exemption. All that time, I never got the Chinese virus.
